arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-45ab669473a744dd8663a3674859eef0 && dotnet test --help 2>/dev/null | grep -i hotreload -A2 -B2 || true
arguments.description: Check dotnet test hot reload options

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check dotnet test hot reload options

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to enable hot reload (dotnet watch) for their test project permanently without setting an environment variable each time. For `dotnet test`, hot reload is done via `dotnet watch test`. But the user mentions not wanting to set an environment variable every time.

For .NET test projects using `dotnet watch`, there's the `DOTNET_WATCH` environment variable, but more likely they're talking about `DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER` or the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` variable.

Actually, with MSTest and the testing platform, hot reload for tests can be enabled via the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable. 

The cleanest solution is to set this permanently through a `.runsettings` file or by configuring MSBuild properties in the `.csproj` or `Directory.Build.props`, so they don't have to pass the environment variable manually each time they run tests. I'm realizing the Microsoft Testing Platform supports hot reload through the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable, and the cleanest way to make this persistent in the project would be to set it via MSBuild configuration rather than relying on CLI flags or external environment variables. I could set the `RunSettingsFilePath` property in the project file to point to the `.runsettings` file, but there's actually a more direct approach using the `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` property to pass the `--hotreload` flag. Though I'm second-guessing whether that property is the right one—the most reliable method is definitely the `.runsettings` file with environment variables, since that's the documented way to configure the testing platform.

Wait, I should clarify what the user actually means by "hot reload." They might be referring to `dotnet watch test`, which automatically reruns tests when files change, rather than the .NET hot reload feature itself. With `dotnet watch`, you'd typically configure it through `launchSettings.json`, but that doesn't require an environment variable. Let me reconsider their actual request—they want hot reload enabled by default when running tests from the command line without manually setting an environment variable each time.

The practical solution is to persist the configuration in the project itself. For the testing platform's hot reload feature, I can set `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED=1` either through a `.runsettings` file or directly in the `.csproj` using a property like `TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled`. I keep circling back to the same solutions. The cleanest approach is setting `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED=1` through a `.runsettings` file and configuring the project to use it, since that's the standard way to handle this with MSTest and the Testing Platform. Let me verify if there's an MSBuild property that might make this even simpler. it in the project file. Actually, I'm realizing there might be a simpler approach using `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` to pass the `--hotreload` flag directly, but I'm not confident about that property name. The most reliable path is the `.runsettings` file with environment variables in the `RunConfiguration` section, which is well-documented and I know will work.

Wait, though—setting the environment variable through `.runsettings` might be too late since the testing platform needs to initialize with that flag already set. Let me think about using a `Directory.Build.targets` file instead, or just setting it directly in the `.csproj` as an MSBuild property that gets translated to the right format.

Actually, I should check what properties are available for the Microsoft Testing Platform integration. With `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport=true`, running `dotnet test` uses the testing platform underneath, and `--hotreload` is a valid testing platform option. But I'm going back and forth on whether a `testingplatform.config.json` file would be cleaner, or if I should just stick with the `.runsettings` approach with environment variables since that's the most established pattern in .NET.

The user mentioned they already know about the environment variable and don't want to set it every time, so I should create a `.runsettings` file that bakes in the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` variable and update the `.csproj` to reference it. The testing platform's hot reload can be enabled with the `--hotreload` flag or by setting `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED=1`. Since their project has testing platform support enabled, they're likely looking to make this environment variable permanent in their MSBuild configuration.

I'm considering a few approaches: using a `.runsettings` file with environment variables, creating a wrapper script, or leveraging a `.testingplatformconfig.json` file. The `.runsettings` approach seems cleanest since it integrates directly with the test runner without depending on build output paths.

Before committing to that, I should verify whether hot reload is controlled by `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` in the newer Microsoft.Testing.Platform or if there's a different mechanism. Let me check what the dotnet SDK actually supports and what options are available for `dotnet test` directly.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Description:
  
  
  Environment variables:
  
    DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will poll the file system for
    changes. This is required for some file systems, such as network shares,
    Docker mounted volumes, and other virtual file systems.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' on all child processes launched.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_ITERATION
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' and increments by one each time
    a file is changed and the command is restarted.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_SUPPRESS_EMOJIS
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will not show emojis in the 
    console output.
  
  Remarks:
    The special option '--' is used to delimit the end of the options and
    the beginning of arguments that will be passed to the child dotnet process.
  
    For example: dotnet watch -- --verbose run
  
    Even though '--verbose' is an option dotnet-watch supports, the use of '--'
    indicates that '--verbose' should be treated instead as an argument for
    dotnet-run.
  
  Examples:
    dotnet watch run
    dotnet watch test
  
      

Usage:
  dotnet-watch [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]
<exited with exit code 0>

                      Condition=" '$(EnableMicrosoftTestingExtensionsHotReload)' == 'true' " />

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Check for command line argument properties

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check for command line argument properties

data.reasoningText:
Now I see it. The MSTest SDK has a hot reload extension. The property is `EnableMicrosoftTestingExtensionsHotReload` and it's set to `true` when `TestingExtensionsProfile` is `AllMicrosoft`. But this project is using `Microsoft.NET.Sdk` with MSTest packages, not the `MSTest.Sdk`.

The hot reload extension is `Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.HotReload`. To enable it, this project needs to either:
1. Switch to MSTest SDK (which is a bigger change)
2. Add the `Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.HotReload` package reference directly


3. Or check if there's a simpler way through the `dotnet test --hotreload` command line option

Looking at the `ClassicEngine.targets` file, the MSTest SDK can automatically include this package, but since this project is using `Microsoft.NET.Sdk` with the testing platform support enabled, I'd need to add the hot reload extension package directly as a NuGet reference.

The challenge is that just adding the package still requires either passing the `--hotreload` flag or setting the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED=1` environment variable each time, which the user wants to avoid. Let me see if there's a way to configure default command-line arguments or auto-enable this through the testing platform configuration. I could also add the `Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.HotReload` package and configure it through a `.runsettings` file linked in the project, or more directly by setting an MSBuild property that automatically passes the `--hotreload` flag to the test runner.

data.reasoningText:
OK so the `Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.HotReload` package registers a builder hook. It needs to be added as a package reference. But the user also needs to enable it without setting the environment variable each time.

Let me check the `dotnet test` integration targets to see how hot reload is invoked.

arguments.description: Check Test SDK targets for hot reload

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "find ~/.nuget/packages/microsoft.net.test.sdk/17.13.0/ -name \"*.targets\" -o -name \"*.props\" 2>/dev/null | head -10 && grep -ri \"hotreload\\|HOTRELOAD\" ~/.nuget/packages/microsoft.net.test.sdk/17.13.0/ --include=\"*.targets\" --include=\"*.props\" 2>/dev/null", "description": "Check Test SDK targets for hot reload", "initial_wait": 10}

toolCalls.name: bash

data.reasoningText:
OK, so `SupportsHotReload` is a project capability that's already supported for .NET 6+ projects. This is about IDE hot reload support, not test-specific hot reload.

Let me think about this differently. The user wants to use `dotnet watch test` for hot reload during test development, or they want the Testing Platform's `--hotreload` to be always on.

Actually, maybe the simplest approach is to just add the `Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.HotReload` package and then create a `.runsettings` file that sets the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED=1` environment variable. 

But wait, I'm realizing the `.runsettings` environment variables might be applied too late in the process—the testing platform reads the hot reload flag at startup, before those variables take effect. Let me explore using `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` instead to see if that's a viable way to pass the flag directly.

toolCalls.args: {"command": "grep -ri \"TestingPlatformCommandLine\\|TestingPlatformArgs\\|AdditionalTestArguments\\|TestRunParameters\\|TestAdaptersPaths\" /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.312/ --include=\"*.targets\" --include=\"*.props\" 2>/dev/null | grep -i \"test\" | head -10", "description": "Search for test argument properties", "initial_wait": 10}

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cat /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.312/Current/Microsoft.Common.targets/ImportAfter/Microsoft.TestPlatform.ImportAfter.targets", "description": "Read TestPlatform targets", "initial_wait": 10}

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
<!--
***********************************************************************************************
Microsoft.TestPlatform.ImportAfter.targets

WARNING:  DO NOT MODIFY this file unless you are knowledgeable about MSBuild and have
          created a backup copy.  Incorrect changes to this file will make it
          impossible to load or build your projects from the command-line or the IDE.

Copyright (c) .NET Foundation. All rights reserved.
***********************************************************************************************
-->

<Project xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/developer/msbuild/2003">
  <!-- Import Microsoft.TestPlatform.targets for VSTest target -->
  <PropertyGroup>
    <MSBuildAllProjects>$(MSBuildAllProjects);$(MSBuildThisFileFullPath)</MSBuildAllProjects>
    <VSTestTargets Condition="'$(VSTestTargets)'==''">$(MSBuildExtensionsPath)\Microsoft.TestPlatform.targets</VSTestTargets>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <Import Condition="Exists('$(VSTestTargets)')" Project="$(VSTestTargets)" />
  <!-- Register TestingPlatform task -->
  <UsingTask TaskName="GetTestsProject" AssemblyFile="$(MicrosoftNETBuildTasksDirectory)Microsoft.NET.Build.Tasks.dll" />
  <Target Name="_GetTestsProject" Condition=" $(IsTestProject) == 'true' OR '$(IsTestingPlatformApplication)' == 'true' " >
    <GetTestsProject TargetPath="$(TargetPath)" GetTestsProjectPipeName="$(GetTestsProjectPipeName)" ProjectFullPath="$(MSBuildProjectFullPath)" />
  </Target>
</Project>
<exited with exit code 0>
